stderr: Reading additional input from stdin...

I’m taking over the active-work window from the supplied snapshot. I’ll first verify the live Refine status and the known provider-size failure, then keep polling and apply only recovery actions already supported by Refine’s auditable workflow path. I’ll surface any authority boundary instead of crossing it.

Live evidence is newer than the attached snapshot: the daemon and both workflow/Git-sync workers are running, and six managed goal-agent processes have launched successfully. That means the earlier `E2BIG` provider failure remains an auditable historical issue, but it is not currently preventing dispatch. I’m leaving those agents and their worktrees untouched while I inspect status through the running daemon.

The active window is healthy enough to continue without intervention: all six slots are claimed under the configured 10-agent limits, the website returns 200, daemon/workflow/Git-sync processes are alive, memory and disk have ample headroom, and no current authentication failure is visible. The only mild runtime pressure is expected build load; I’m now watching state transitions and agent exits for stalls, loss, or provider errors.

All six leases remain live and their output files are advancing; several agents are already running focused tests. One goal-agent hit a test failure, but it is still actively investigating, so there is no stall or loss signal and no justification to retry or mutate workflow state yet.

Two goals have cleanly handed off from implementation to Refine’s governance stage, including the retry for duplicated supervisor events. Refine replaced the implementation subprocesses with governance subprocesses under the same workflow executions; leases remain intact, so I’m preserving the normal validation path.

Four of the six queued goals have now completed Refine’s full implementation → governance → publish/build/quality path and are in review. Two remain in progress: the provider-argument failure fix is in validation, and the parallel-agent visibility/capacity fix is still implementing. No goal has failed in this active window.

A user-driven bulk approval partially succeeded: 8 of 10 review goals merged, while two remained in review because Git reported real content conflicts. I will not force or auto-resolve those merges. I’m checking whether the failed approvals left the primary checkout or any Refine-owned merge worktree in a conflicted state, while the final active goal continues governance.

The queue has reached idle, but not cleanly: the last goal failed during its post-implementation path. I’m preserving that failure and gathering the exact stage/evidence before deciding whether Refine exposes a bounded retry; I will not relabel it as success or bypass governance. The two approval conflicts also remain safely in review, with the primary checkout clean and aligned to `origin/main`.

The final failure is a governance gate, not a broken implementation or provider issue: code/tests passed, but the required Workflow Engine intent documentation was omitted. I’m using Refine’s recovery-round mechanism to address exactly that documented violation; the candidate remains isolated, and the eventual merge still requires normal review/approval.

New user steering arrived through Refine itself: the two conflicted review goals were given follow-up rounds and are now actively reconciling against current `main`. My governance recovery is queued behind them. Its temporary `todo` state reproduces the exact scheduler defect under repair—the current daemon does not replenish a running batch—so I’m letting the singleton workflow runner finish the two active reconciliations and claim it normally afterward.

A new user Goal entered the same active window: command-palette completeness, currently `todo`. Both it and the governance recovery are queued behind the two live reconciliation agents because the running scheduler still has the replenishment defect. I’m including the new Goal in this supervision window and keeping the existing runner authoritative.

The first reconciliation batch completed successfully: both goals are back in review, and Refine then claimed the waiting scheduler-governance recovery plus the new command-palette Goal as the next batch. This confirms the old runner’s batch-boundary behavior without any manual dispatch or duplicate engine.
